description: The image runs with SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod (Dockerfile line 26). The prod profile relocates the actuator management server to a separate port: application-prod.yaml lines 69-70 set `management.server.port: ${MANAGEMENT_PORT:8081}`, while `server.port` is 8080 (line 26). Actuator endpoints (health/info/prometheus) are therefore served on 8081, NOT 8080. The container HEALTHCHECK probes http://localhost:8080/actuator/health, which does not exist on the app port — the health probe will always return a non-2xx/connection result and the container will be perpetually reported unhealthy. The UAT profile (application-uat.yaml lines 64-65) relocates it identically, so any profile actually used in the image is broken.
recommendation: Point the HEALTHCHECK at the management port: `curl -sf http://localhost:8081/actuator/health` (or 127.0.0.1:${MANAGEMENT_PORT:-8081}). Keep it aligned with the management.server.port used by the active profile, and EXPOSE 8081 as well.
verification_note: Snippet matches Dockerfile:31-32 exactly. Defect holds: Dockerfile:26 sets SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod; application-prod.yaml:69-70 relocate the actuator to management.server.port ${MANAGEMENT_PORT:8081} while server.port is 8080 (line 26), so /actuator/health is NOT served on 8080. The HEALTHCHECK CMD curl http://localhost:8080/actuator/health therefore fails and the container is reported perpetually unhealthy. UAT profile (application-uat.yaml:64-65) relocates identically. Claim is technically accurate. Downgraded from major to minor because the stated production deploy path is Argo CD/k8s (workflow comments confirm kustomize overlays + Argo CD Image Updater), and Kubernetes ignores the Docker image HEALTHCHECK directive entirely in favor of its own liveness/readiness probes — so the broken directive has no effect on the actual deployment and only bites plain-docker/compose/local runs. Real misconfig, muted blast radius.

snippet:
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
...
  CMD curl -sf http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1
description: The HEALTHCHECK invokes `curl`, but the eclipse-temurin:21-jre base (Ubuntu-based JRE) does not ship curl by default — no `apt-get install curl` step exists in this Dockerfile. When curl is absent the healthcheck command exits 127 ('curl: not found'), which Docker treats as unhealthy, so the probe fails regardless of application state. This compounds finding #1: even after the port is corrected the probe cannot succeed without the binary.
recommendation: Either install curl (`RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`) or, preferably, avoid the extra binary by using a JVM/wget-free probe — e.g. a Spring Boot readiness probe checked by the orchestrator instead of a container HEALTHCHECK, or add a tiny health client. Verify the chosen tool exists in the final image.

description: The Java runtime base image is pinned only to a floating major tag `21-jre`, with no minor/patch version and no digest. `21-jre` moves as new Temurin builds and, more importantly, as the underlying Ubuntu base is rebased, so identical `docker build` invocations produce non-reproducible images and can silently pull in an updated OS layer. The charter calls for pinning by tag (ideally digest). The Node image (economy-explorer/Dockerfile) pins to `node:22-alpine` similarly without a digest.
recommendation: Pin to a specific point release and ideally a digest, e.g. `FROM eclipse-temurin:21.0.x_y-jre-jammy@sha256:...`. Apply the same to economy-explorer/Dockerfile's `node:22-alpine` (pin `node:22.x.y-alpine@sha256:...`). Bump deliberately via Dependabot/Renovate rather than drifting on a floating tag.

occurrences.snippet:
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
...
FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
...
FROM node:22-alpine AS runner

snippet:
- name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          IMAGE=${{ secrets.HARBOR_REGISTRY }}/paradaux-public/treasury-rest-api
          docker build \
            -t "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.SHA_TAG }}" \
            -t "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.MUTABLE_TAG }}" \
            treasury-rest-api
description: Neither image-build workflow performs vulnerability scanning of the built image before pushing to Harbor. The charter explicitly asks to flag missing image vulnerability scanning. Both the treasury-rest-api (money service) and economy-explorer images are built and pushed straight to the registry with no Trivy/Grype/Harbor-scan gate, so a base-image CVE (compounded by the unpinned floating bases in finding #3) ships to production overlays via Argo CD Image Updater with no check.
recommendation: Add an image-scan step (e.g. aquasecurity/trivy-action on the locally-built tag with `exit-code: 1` and a severity threshold) between build and push, or rely on Harbor's built-in scanner with a 'prevent vulnerable images from running' policy. Fail the workflow on HIGH/CRITICAL findings.

- name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          IMAGE=${{ secrets.HARBOR_REGISTRY }}/paradaux-public/economy-explorer
          docker build \

            -t "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.MUTABLE_TAG }}" \
            .

snippet:
- name: Set up Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: economy-explorer/package-lock.json
description: Node version drift across the explorer's own toolchain. The Dockerfile builds on `node:22-alpine` (economy-explorer/Dockerfile lines 5/10/17) and economy-explorer-ci.yml uses `node-version: 22` (line 56), but the schema-drift gate runs `npm ci` and `npm run db:codegen` under `node-version: 20`. Running the codegen / dependency install on a different major than the production build image can mask engine-incompatibility warnings and produce lockfile/behaviour differences that only surface in the real (node 22) build.
recommendation: Bump this workflow's `node-version` to 22 to match the Dockerfile and CI. Better, centralize the version via an `.nvmrc`/`.node-version` file and set `node-version-file:` in every setup-node step so it can't drift.

snippet:
- name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          IMAGE=${{ secrets.HARBOR_REGISTRY }}/paradaux-public/treasury-rest-api
          docker build \
            -t "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.SHA_TAG }}" \
            -t "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.MUTABLE_TAG }}" \
            treasury-rest-api
description: The Harbor push workflows use raw `docker build`/`docker push` rather than docker/build-push-action, and the image build/push steps are not pinned to action SHAs — but more concretely, the workflows push a mutable tag (`prod`/`latest`) alongside the immutable `<env>-sha-<short>` tag on every build. The immutable sha tag is good; however there is no provenance/SBOM attestation and no `--provenance`/`--sbom` emission, so the pushed money-service image carries no supply-chain metadata for the immutable tag Argo CD deploys. Combined with no scan (finding #4), a compromised or vulnerable layer has no detection surface.
recommendation: Switch to docker/build-push-action@<pinned-sha> with `provenance: true` and `sbom: true`, or add a buildx step emitting an SBOM attestation, so each immutable sha tag has verifiable provenance. Keep the sha tag as the deploy target (already the case).

occurrences.snippet:
- name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          IMAGE=${{ secrets.HARBOR_REGISTRY }}/paradaux-public/economy-explorer
          docker build ...
      - name: Push Docker image
        run: |
          docker push "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.SHA_TAG }}"
          docker push "${IMAGE}:${{ steps.tags.outputs.MUTABLE_TAG }}"

verification_note: Snippet matches workflow lines 99-105 exactly (Build Docker image step uses raw docker build with -t SHA_TAG and -t MUTABLE_TAG). Claims verified against the file: uses raw docker build/docker push (lines 99-111) not build-push-action; pushes both an immutable ENV-sha-<short> tag and a mutable prod/latest tag on every build (tags computed lines 78-90, pushed lines 107-111); no --provenance/--sbom flags and no attestation anywhere in the workflow. So the money-service image carries no supply-chain metadata. Accurate, appropriately scoped supply-chain observation; minor is fair for a solo-dev recoverable-money game economy with no infra control over the registry.
